62	---
63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. 1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115

121	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
122	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
123	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
124	  % L2282-L2297).
125	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
126	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
127	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
128	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
129	  %  $$
130	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
131	  %  $$
132	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
133	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
134	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.

164	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}

217	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
218	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
219	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
220	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
221	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
222	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
223	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
224	  the sheafification adjunction
225	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
226	  presheaves of modules, and write
227	  \[
228	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
229	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
230	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
231	  \]
232	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
233	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
234	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
235	  \[
236	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
237	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
238	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
239	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
240	  \]
241	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
242	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
243	\end{lemma}
244	
245	\begin{proof}
246	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
247	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
248	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
249	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
250	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
251	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
252	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
253	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
254	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
255	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
256	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
257	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
258	
259	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
260	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
261	  is the objectwise relative tensor
262	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
263	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
264	  maps
265	  \[
266	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
267	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
268	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
269	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
270	  \]
271	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
272	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
273	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
274	  pair
275	  \[
276	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
277	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
278	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
279	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
280	  \]
281	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
282	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
283	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
284	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
285	
286	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
287	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
288	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
289	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
290	  of \(a\)-images. Now the localization unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}}\) lies in
291	  \(J.W\), and the class \(J.W\) is \emph{monoidal} for the
292	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor: whiskering a local isomorphism by
293	  \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) again yields a local isomorphism. (This is the
294	  abelian monoidality of \(J.W\), itself proved through the fact that the
295	  internal hom into a sheaf is again a sheaf.) Consequently the two whiskered
296	  comparison maps
297	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) and
298	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
299	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) relating the two rows are
300	  isomorphisms.
301	
302	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
303	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
304	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
305	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
306	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
307	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
308	\end{proof}

310	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
311	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
312	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
313	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
314	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
315	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
316	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
317	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
318	  \[
319	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
320	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
321	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
322	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
323	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
324	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
325	  \]
326	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
327	  arguments.
328	\end{corollary}
329	
330	\begin{proof}
331	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
332	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
333	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
334	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
335	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
336	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
337	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
338	  \[
339	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
340	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
341	    \qquad
342	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
343	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
344	  \]
345	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
346	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
347	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
348	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
349	  \[
350	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
351	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
352	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
353	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
354	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
355	  \]
356	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
357	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
358	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
359	  morphism
360	  \[
361	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
362	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
363	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
364	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
365	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
366	  \]
367	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
368	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
369	  \[
370	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
371	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
372	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
373	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
374	  \]
375	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
376	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
377	  composite
378	  \[
379	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
380	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
381	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
382	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
383	  \]
384	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
385	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
386	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
387	\end{proof}

389	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
390	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
392	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
393	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
394	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
395	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
396	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
397	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
398	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
399	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
400	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
401	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
402	  %  (formulation omitted)."
403	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
404	  power isomorphisms).}
405	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
406	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
407	  \[
408	    \mu_{m,m'} :
409	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
410	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
411	  \]
412	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
413	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
414	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
415	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
416	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
417	\end{lemma}
418	
419	\begin{proof}
420	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
421	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
422	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
423	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
424	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
425	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
426	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
427	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
428	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
429	
430	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
431	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
432	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
433	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
434	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
435	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
436	  sheaf tensor products
437	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
438	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
439	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
440	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
441	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
442	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
443	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
444	  of the tensor product is never used.
445	
446	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
447	  \(m = 0\),
448	  \[
449	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
450	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
451	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
452	  \]
453	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
454	  recursive definition
455	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
456	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
457	  \[
458	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
459	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
460	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
461	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
462	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
463	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
464	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
465	  \]
466	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
467	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
468	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
469	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
470	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
471	  \[
472	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
473	      \mathcal{L}
474	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
475	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
476	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
477	  \]
478	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
479	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
480	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
481	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
482	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
483	
484	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
485	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
486	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
487	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
488	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
489	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
490	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
491	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
492	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
493	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
494	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
495	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
496	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
497	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
498	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
499	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
500	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
501	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
502	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
503	\end{proof}

505	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
506	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
507	
508	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
509	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
510	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
511	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
512	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
513	global sections.
514	
515	\begin{definition}\leanok
516	[Section multiplication]
517	  \label{def:sectionMul}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
519	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
520	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
521	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
522	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
523	  \[
524	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
525	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
526	    \;\longrightarrow\;
527	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
528	  \]
529	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
530	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
531	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
532	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
533	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
534	  the sheafification unit
535	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
536	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
537	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
538	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
539	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
540	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
541	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
542	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
543	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
544	\end{definition}
545	
546	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
547	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
548	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
549	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
550	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
551	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
552	  the two composites
553	  \[
554	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
555	    \longrightarrow
556	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
557	    \cong
558	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
559	  \]
560	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
561	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
562	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
563	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
564	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
565	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
566	\end{lemma}
567	
568	\begin{proof}
569	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
570	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
571	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
572	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
573	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
574	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
575	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
576	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
577	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
578	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
579	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
580	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
581	  section multiplications.
582	\end{proof}

584	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
585	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
586	
587	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
588	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
589	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
590	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
591	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
592	
593	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
594	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
595	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
596	  \mathlibok
597	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
598	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
599	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
600	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
601	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
602	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
603	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
604	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
605	  \(1 \in A_0\).
606	\end{lemma}
607	
608	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
609	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
610	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
611	  \mathlibok
612	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
613	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
614	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
615	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
616	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
617	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
618	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
619	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
620	  extension of the graded action.
621	\end{lemma}

623	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
624	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
625	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
626	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
627	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
628	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
629	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",

682	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]

720	  \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add} and the symmetry of the tensor product).
721	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
722	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
723	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
724	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
725	\end{lemma}
726	
727	\begin{proof}
728	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
729	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
730	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---
731	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication
732	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
733	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of
734	  the section multiplication (\cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}) together with the
735	  associativity and commutativity constraints of the comparison isomorphisms
736	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), exactly as in the proof of
737	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring} but with the third tensor factor
738	  carrying \(\mathcal{F}_s\). These are the axioms of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), so
739	  \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib} produces the module structure of
740	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over \(R(X_s, L_s)\).
741	\end{proof}

1	import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal
2	import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification
3	import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf

5	/-!
6	# Section graded ring infrastructure, Layer 1: tensor powers of a sheaf of modules
7	
8	This file builds the Mathlib-absent infrastructure of
9	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_SectionGradedRing.tex`, Layer 1
10	(`sec:sgr_tensor_powers`): the tensor product, tensor powers, and twists of
11	sheaves of modules over a scheme `X`, together with the unitor and braiding
12	isomorphisms of the sheaf tensor product.
13	
14	The category `X.Modules = SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf` of sheaves of modules
15	over a scheme carries **no** monoidal structure in Mathlib (the structure sheaf
16	varies the base ring over opens).  Mathlib *does* supply:
17	
18	* the symmetric monoidal structure on the category of **presheaves** of modules
19	  `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`
20	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal`), and
21	* the sheafification functor `PresheafOfModules.sheafification`
22	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification`).
23	
24	We therefore build the tensor product of sheaves of modules as the sheafification
25	of the objectwise (presheaf) tensor product, following
26	[Stacks, Tag 01CA].
27	
28	## Main definitions
29	
30	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification` — the scheme-level
31	  sheafification functor `X.PresheafOfModules ⥤ X.Modules`.
32	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` (`def:sheafTensorObj`) —
33	  `F ⊗ G := (F.toPresheaf ⊗ G.toPresheaf)^#`.
34	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow` (`def:sheafTensorPow`) —
35	  the `m`-th tensor power `L^{⊗m}` of a sheaf of modules.
36	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow` (`def:sheafModuleTwist`) —
37	  the `m`-twist `F(m) = F ⊗ L^{⊗m}`.
38	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCounitIso` — the reflective
39	  counit iso `(F.toPresheaf)^# ≅ F`.
40	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjUnitIso`,
41	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor`,
42	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding` — the left/right unitor and
43	  braiding isomorphisms of the sheaf tensor product.
44	
45	The comparison isomorphism `L^{⊗m} ⊗ L^{⊗m'} ≅ L^{⊗(m+m')}`
46	(`lem:sheafTensorPow_add`) is **deferred**: see the handoff note before the end
47	of the file for the single missing ingredient (strong-monoidality of the module
48	sheafification functor) and the launching pad assembled here.
49	-/
50	
51	universe u
52	
53	open CategoryTheory MonoidalCategory Limits
54	
55	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules
56	
57	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
58	
59	/-- The scheme-level sheafification functor, sending a presheaf of modules over a
60	scheme `X` to its associated sheaf of modules `X.Modules`.  It is the
61	`PresheafOfModules.sheafification` functor for the identity morphism of the
62	underlying presheaf of rings (which is locally bijective). -/
63	private noncomputable def sheafification : X.PresheafOfModules ⥤ X.Modules :=
64	  PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)
65	
66	/-- The category `X.PresheafOfModules` of presheaves of modules over a scheme,
67	presented in the exact form `PresheafOfModules (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)`
68	for which Mathlib equips it with a symmetric monoidal structure.  This is
69	*definitionally* `X.PresheafOfModules` (since
70	`X.ringCatSheaf.obj = X.sheaf.obj ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat`), so a term of
71	either type is accepted for the other. -/
72	private abbrev MonoidalPresheaf (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type _ :=
73	  _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (X.sheaf.obj ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)
74	
75	/-- The tensor product of two sheaves of modules over a scheme, defined as the
76	sheafification of the objectwise tensor product presheaf
77	(Mathlib `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`).  See [Stacks, Tag 01CA]
78	(`def:sheafTensorObj`). -/
79	noncomputable def tensorObj (F G : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
80	  sheafification.obj
81	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
82	      ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G))
83	
84	/-- The structure sheaf as a sheaf of modules over itself: the unit object of the
85	tensor product, i.e. the zeroth tensor power `L^{⊗0} = 𝒪_X`
86	(`lem:moduleUnit_mathlib`). -/
87	private noncomputable abbrev unitModule (X : Scheme.{u}) : X.Modules :=
88	  SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf
89	
90	/-- The `m`-th tensor power `L^{⊗m}` of a sheaf of modules over a scheme, defined
91	by recursion: `L^{⊗0} = 𝒪_X` (the unit module) and
92	`L^{⊗(m+1)} = L^{⊗m} ⊗ L`.  See [Stacks, Tag 01CU] (`def:sheafTensorPow`). -/
93	noncomputable def tensorPow (L : X.Modules) : ℕ → X.Modules
94	  | 0 => unitModule X
95	  | (m + 1) => tensorObj (tensorPow L m) L
96	
97	@[simp] private lemma tensorPow_zero (L : X.Modules) : tensorPow L 0 = unitModule X := rfl
98	
99	@[simp] private lemma tensorPow_succ (L : X.Modules) (m : ℕ) :
100	    tensorPow L (m + 1) = tensorObj (tensorPow L m) L := rfl
101	
102	/-- The `m`-twist `F(m) = F ⊗ L^{⊗m}` of a sheaf of modules `F` by the `m`-th
103	tensor power of a line bundle `L` (`def:sheafModuleTwist`).  This is the
104	degree-`m` carrier of the section graded module. -/
105	noncomputable def moduleTensorPow (F L : X.Modules) (m : ℕ) : X.Modules :=
106	  tensorObj F (tensorPow L m)
107	
108	@[simp] private lemma moduleTensorPow_zero (F L : X.Modules) :
109	    moduleTensorPow F L 0 = tensorObj F (unitModule X) := rfl
110	
111	/-! ### Unitor and braiding isomorphisms of the sheaf tensor product
112	
113	These are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal structure on `X.Modules`
114	that descend through sheafification from `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`
115	using only *functoriality* of `sheafification` (and, for the unitors, the
116	reflective counit iso) — no strong-monoidality of `sheafification` is needed, so
117	they are axiom-clean.  They are the launching pad for `tensorPowAdd`. -/
118	
119	/-- The counit isomorphism of the module sheafification adjunction: sheafifying
120	the underlying presheaf of a sheaf of modules returns the sheaf itself.  This is
121	an isomorphism because the counit of `sheafification ⊣ toPresheafOfModules` is
122	invertible (the right adjoint `SheafOfModules.forget` is fully faithful).  It is
123	the launching pad for the left-unitor base case of `tensorPowAdd`. -/
124	private noncomputable def sheafificationCounitIso (G : X.Modules) :
125	    sheafification.obj ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G) ≅ G :=
126	  (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction
127	    (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).counit).app G
128	
129	/-- The left-unitor isomorphism `unitModule X ⊗ G ≅ G` of the sheaf tensor
130	product: the presheaf left unitor `λ_` descended through sheafification, composed
131	with the counit iso `sheafificationCounitIso`.  This is the base case (`m = 0`) of
132	`tensorPowAdd`.  Axiom-clean. -/
133	private noncomputable def tensorObjUnitIso (G : X.Modules) :
134	    tensorObj (unitModule X) G ≅ G :=
135	  sheafification.mapIso
136	      (MonoidalCategory.leftUnitor (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
137	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G)) ≪≫
138	    sheafificationCounitIso G
139	
140	/-- The right-unitor isomorphism `G ⊗ unitModule X ≅ G` of the sheaf tensor
141	product: the presheaf right unitor `ρ_` descended through sheafification, composed
142	with the counit iso `sheafificationCounitIso`.  Axiom-clean (no monoidal structure
143	on `X.Modules` is required). -/
144	private noncomputable def tensorObjRightUnitor (G : X.Modules) :
145	    tensorObj G (unitModule X) ≅ G :=
146	  sheafification.mapIso
147	      (MonoidalCategory.rightUnitor (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
148	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G)) ≪≫
149	    sheafificationCounitIso G
150	
151	/-- The braiding isomorphism `F ⊗ G ≅ G ⊗ F` of the sheaf tensor product,
152	descended through sheafification from the symmetric braiding on
153	`X.PresheafOfModules` (`PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`).  Axiom-clean: the
154	braiding is pure sheafification-functoriality of the presheaf-level braiding, so
155	no monoidal structure on `X.Modules` is required.  This is the symmetry used in
156	the inductive step of `tensorPowAdd`. -/
157	private noncomputable def tensorBraiding (F G : X.Modules) :
158	    tensorObj F G ≅ tensorObj G F :=
159	  sheafification.mapIso
160	    (BraidedCategory.braiding (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
161	      ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G))
162	
163	/-! ### Lax-monoidal global sections: the section multiplication
164	
165	The global-sections functor `Γ(X, -)` is only *lax* monoidal: a pair of global
166	sections does not commute with sheafification, so the multiplication is a map,
167	not an isomorphism.  It is nonetheless `Γ(X, 𝒪_X)`-linear and is the data the
168	section graded ring is built from. -/
169	
170	/-- The **section multiplication** (`def:sectionMul`), the `Γ(X,𝒪_X)`-bilinear map
171	`Γ(X,F) ⊗_{Γ(X,𝒪_X)} Γ(X,G) → Γ(X, F ⊗ G)`.
172	
173	Its domain `(F.toPresheaf ⊗ G.toPresheaf)(X)` is, by the objectwise formula of
174	`PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, the `Γ(X,𝒪_X)`-module
175	`Γ(X,F) ⊗_{Γ(X,𝒪_X)} Γ(X,G)` of elementary tensors of global sections; a pair
176	`(σ, τ)` is sent to `σ ⊗ τ`.  Postcomposing with the global-sections component of
177	the sheafification unit `η : P → P^#` (`def:sheafTensorObj`) lands in
178	`Γ(X, F ⊗ G)`.  As a morphism in `ModuleCat (Γ(X,𝒪_X))` it is automatically
179	`Γ(X,𝒪_X)`-bilinear; this records that linearity.  Axiom-clean: it is pure
180	sheafification-unit naturality, requiring no monoidal structure on `X.Modules`. -/
181	noncomputable def sectionsMul (F G : X.Modules) :
182	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
183	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G)).obj (Opposite.op ⊤) ⟶
184	      (tensorObj F G).val.obj (Opposite.op ⊤) :=
185	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).unit.app
186	      (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
187	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G))).app (Opposite.op ⊤)
188	
189	/-
190	### The tensor-power comparison isomorphism `tensorPowAdd` — DEFERRED (handoff)
191	
192	The canonical comparison isomorphism (`lem:sheafTensorPow_add`, [Stacks, Tag 01CU])
193	
194	  `tensorPowAdd (L : X.Modules) (m m' : ℕ) :`
195	  `  tensorObj (tensorPow L m) (tensorPow L m') ≅ tensorPow L (m + m')`
196	
197	is **not** provided in this iteration.  Per the `mathlib-build` discipline it is
198	left *absent* rather than backed by a `sorry`.  Its proof is by induction on `m`:
199	
200	* **base case `m = 0`** — FULLY AVAILABLE, axiom-clean:
201	  `tensorObjUnitIso (tensorPow L m') ≪≫ eqToIso (by rw [Nat.zero_add])`
202	  (left unitor `unitModule X ⊗ L^{⊗m'} ≅ L^{⊗m'}`, transported along `0 + m' = m'`).
203	
204	* **inductive step `m = k+1`** — needs the sheaf-level **associator**
205	  `tensorObj (tensorObj A B) C ≅ tensorObj A (tensorObj B C)`.  Concretely, with
206	  `A = L^{⊗k}`, `B = L^{⊗m'}`, one must produce
207	  `(A ⊗ L) ⊗ B ≅ (A ⊗ B) ⊗ L` (= associator, then `tensorBraiding`, then
208	  associator⁻¹), combine with the inductive hypothesis `L^{⊗(k+m')} ≅ A ⊗ B`
209	  and `Nat.succ_add`.  Everything here EXCEPT the associator is already built
210	  (`tensorBraiding`, `tensorPow_succ`).
211	
212	THE SINGLE MISSING INGREDIENT is the associator, equivalently the
213	**strong-monoidality of the module sheafification functor**
214	`sheafification : X.PresheafOfModules ⥤ X.Modules`: the canonical maps
215	`sheafification.obj (P ⊗ Q) ⟶ sheafification.obj ((sheafification.obj P).val ⊗ Q)`
216	— obtained by applying `sheafification` to `η_P ⊗ 𝟙_Q`, where
217	`η = (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 _)).unit` — are isomorphisms.
218	This holds because `η_P ⊗ 𝟙_Q` is inverted by sheafification: it is a stalkwise
219	isomorphism (tensor commutes with the filtered-colimit stalks and `η_P` is a
220	stalk iso), even though it need not be locally *injective* (tensoring is only
221	right exact).  Mathlib (pinned commit) supplies the abstract
222	`CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal` machinery but **not** its instantiation for
223	the presheaf-of-modules sheafification localizer, nor a stalkwise-iso criterion
224	for morphisms of sheaves of modules; building either is the next-iteration task.
225	
226	LAUNCHING PAD (all axiom-clean, BUILT ABOVE): `sheafificationCounitIso`,
227	`tensorObjUnitIso`, `tensorObjRightUnitor`, `tensorBraiding`, and now also
228	`sectionsMul` (the lax-monoidal multiplication, which does NOT need the associator).
